step.title: Analysis and Pattern Recognition
step.goal: Analyze source material to identify patterns, conventions, and extractable knowledge. This is the CORE STEP - quality here determines quality of output.
step.note: PATTERN RECOGNITION is the key skill. Look for: - What repeats across examples? - What's consistent vs variable? - What's the "why" behind choices? - What would someone need to know to replicate this?

substep.title: Load Source Material
First Pass - Surface Patterns
substep.action:
Identify obvious patterns on first read:
**Structure patterns:** - How are files/sections organized? - What's the consistent skeleton? - What sections always appear?
**Naming patterns:** - What naming conventions are used? - Are there prefixes/suffixes with meaning? - How are things categorized?
**Format patterns:** - What syntax/format is preferred? - How is information structured? - What markup/styling is used?

substep.title: Second Pass - Deep Patterns
substep.action:
Look deeper for non-obvious patterns:
**Decision patterns:** - When is X used vs Y? - What conditions trigger different approaches? - What trade-offs are being made?
**Quality patterns:** - What makes good examples good? - What do the best examples have in common? - What's missing from weaker examples?
**Context patterns:** - What assumptions are embedded? - What knowledge is assumed? - What would confuse a newcomer?

substep.title: Extract Rationale
substep.action:
For each pattern identified, capture the WHY:
- Why is this pattern used? - What problem does it solve? - What would happen if you didn't follow it? - What are the trade-offs?
Rationale makes knowledge ACTIONABLE, not just descriptive.
substep.ask:
For the patterns identified, what's the reasoning behind them?
Help me understand: - Why these conventions exist - What problems they solve - Historical context if relevant

substep.title: Identify Examples
substep.action:
For each pattern, identify concrete examples:
**Good examples** - Show the pattern done well **Counter-examples** - Show what NOT to do (if available) **Edge cases** - Show pattern in tricky situations
Examples are critical for LLM consumption (few-shot learning).

substep.title: Localize and Verify (fixing an existing agent/skill)

action.if: the target is a defect in an existing agent or skill

substep.action:
Do NOT guess where the fix goes. Dispatch the researcher (Task tool, subagent: researcher) to map the exact source sites in .vera/: - the true-source file(s) for the flaw (never a compiled artifact) - the include/attachment chain (so one edit covers all consumers) - whether one shared file is the leverage point vs many
Then VERIFY the researcher's load-bearing claims with a cheap deterministic probe (a one-off script, a grep, a parse) BEFORE drafting any fix. Treat a dispatched finding as a hypothesis until a probe confirms it.
Output: a localized target list — each item {source file, what's wrong, the precise change}.

substep.title: Present Analysis

techniques.technique: Compare multiple examples side-by-side. What's consistent? What varies?
Remove specifics, see the skeleton. What's the template underneath?
Ask "why" for every choice. The answer reveals the pattern's purpose.
Imagine the opposite. What would break if this pattern wasn't followed?

section.title: Pattern Categories
section.category: How things are organized, ordered, grouped. File layouts, section ordering, hierarchy.
How things are named, labeled, identified. Prefixes, suffixes, conventions, vocabulary.
How things work, interact, respond. Workflows, error handling, state transitions.
How things look, read, feel. Tone, formatting, emphasis, verbosity.

indicators.indicator: Pattern has clear rationale (not just "we do it this way")
Pattern appears in multiple sources (not one-off)
Pattern is actionable (someone could follow it)
Pattern handles edge cases or exceptions

section.title: Common Pitfalls
section.pitfall: Extracting pattern from too few examples. Solution: Require 3+ occurrences before calling it a pattern.
Pattern without rationale is just trivia. Solution: Always capture the "why".
Only seeing obvious patterns, missing deeper ones. Solution: Do multiple passes, ask "why" repeatedly.

example.name: Extracting from Agent Prompts
example.patterns-found: - All start with persona block (role, identity) - All use XML for structure, markdown for prose - All have numbered menu items - All include failure modes / anti-patterns - Emphasis keywords used sparingly (NEVER, MUST, CRITICAL)
example.rationale: Persona first = establishes identity before instructions. XML structure = unambiguous for LLM parsing. Failure modes = prevents common mistakes.

example.name: Extracting from Code
example.patterns-found: - All wrap operations in try/catch - All log errors before returning - All return structured error response - Error codes follow HTTP conventions - User-facing messages separate from debug info
example.rationale: Consistent error handling = predictable API behavior. Separation of messages = security (no internal details to users).
